caveats: a: The latest directly accessible six-digit OEWS mix is May 2022 and excludes self-employed workers.
a: O*NET describes the painter occupation, not firm-level back-office work or the LMM-band population.
a: Task exposure is a judgmental mapping, not an observed AI-substitution rate.
rho: AGC respondents are not a probability sample of painting contractors and may skew larger or more technology-forward.
rho: Census changed its core AI question in late 2025 from production use to any business function, limiting comparability.
rho: Reported tool use is not equivalent to full operational implementation of exposed labor opportunity.
e: No source measures eligibility under this exact lens or EBITDA band.
e: The injected firm count is an estimate derived at the 23832 ancestor level and may include firms whose economics differ from the six-digit lens.
e: Residential repaint, commercial maintenance, industrial coatings, and new-build subcontracting have different repeat profiles.
s5: BizBuySell transactions are voluntarily broker-reported and cover broad small-business categories, not this NAICS/EBITDA lens.
s5: Seller surveys measure intentions and motives rather than completed control transfers.
s5: Owner age, succession readiness, and transferability for the eligible painting-firm population are unobserved.
q: The AGC evidence concerns tariff cost pass-through across construction, the opposite direction of an AI cost saving and a broader population.
q: BizBuySell evidence is broad small-business transaction and pricing sentiment rather than painting contract economics.
q: Retention will vary sharply between homeowner quotes, commercial maintenance agreements, unit-price contracts, and competitively bid subcontracts.
d5: The BLS projection is occupational, national, ten-year, and not a direct output-demand forecast for the six-digit industry.
d5: The horizon is converted mechanically from 10 years to five years and does not model construction-cycle timing.
d5: Demand mix may shift between outsourced contractors, self-employed painters, in-house crews, and homeowner self-service.
o: The range assumes no discontinuous, broadly deployable robotics breakthrough for irregular occupied sites within five years.
o: Large uniform industrial or new-build surfaces may automate faster than residential and maintenance work.
o: This primitive concerns operator requirement, not the number of field labor hours, which may still fall modestly through tools and workflow improvements.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Contractors sell labor-and-material project delivery, usually through a quoted job, bid, unit-price, or subcontract arrangement; repeat work comes from property managers, general contractors, institutional and industrial accounts, and recurring repaint or turnover needs, while homeowner work is more episodic.
lens.excluded_activities: Self-employed individuals without a transferable operating organization, captive in-house painting crews, non-control financing vehicles, roof painting, roadway line painting, automotive painting, wood panel installation, and concrete coating activities classified outside NAICS 238320.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors providing outsourced interior or exterior painting, coatings, surface preparation, and wall-covering installation or removal for residential, commercial, institutional, industrial, or infrastructure customers, including repaint, maintenance, repair, alteration, and new-work jobs.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2022 occupation and wage mix of NAICS 238320 rather than AI-exposed task hours: construction occupations were 80.65% of employment, painters alone 66.22%, while office support was 7.81%, management 5.04%, business and financial operations 3.69%, and sales 0.94%. I map high exposure to clerical, estimating, documentation, scheduling, and sales-support work; moderate exposure to management and supervisory planning; and low exposure to physical painting and preparation. O*NET task descriptions identify both physical core tasks and smaller information tasks. This may overstate substitution where owner-managers perform blended field work and understate it where small firms have unrecorded administrative effort or AI-enabled estimating embedded in craft roles.
caveats: The latest directly accessible six-digit OEWS mix is May 2022 and excludes self-employed workers.
O*NET describes the painter occupation, not firm-level back-office work or the LMM-band population.
Task exposure is a judgmental mapping, not an observed AI-substitution rate.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study of estimator, project-manager, office, sales, and crew-lead hours at 20-30 painting contractors, paired with payroll by role and task-level use of estimating, CRM, dispatch, and generative-AI tools, would materially tighten the wage-weighted exposure range.
a.rationale: The wage-weighted opportunity is bounded by a workforce dominated by on-site manual application and preparation. The credible AI surface is concentrated in estimating, takeoffs, work-order intake, scheduling, customer communications, proposals, bookkeeping, compliance documentation, and purchasing, plus limited decision support for supervisors; it does not include robotically replacing ordinary field painting within five years.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of construction and maintenance painters to rise 4% from 342,200 in 2024 to 355,200 in 2034. Converting that ten-year change to a five-year factor gives about 1.02. I use it as a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service quantity, widening around it because employment also reflects productivity, labor intensity, self-employment, and painters outside NAICS 238320. The employment proxy can understate demand if productivity improves and overstate outsourced-contractor demand if self-service or in-house provision gains share.
caveats: The BLS projection is occupational, national, ten-year, and not a direct output-demand forecast for the six-digit industry.
The horizon is converted mechanically from 10 years to five years and does not model construction-cycle timing.
Demand mix may shift between outsourced contractors, self-employed painters, in-house crews, and homeowner self-service.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar forecast specific to painting-contractor output, separated into residential repaint, commercial turnover/maintenance, industrial/infrastructure coatings, and new construction, from an industry data vendor or a customer-spend panel would replace the employment proxy.
d5.rationale: The central case is nearly flat to modestly higher real service quantity. New construction, property sales and leasing, repaint cycles, maintenance, and an aging building stock sustain work, while cyclicality, financing constraints, office and retail weakness, and homeowner do-it-yourself behavior limit growth.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under this exact lens or EBITDA band.
The injected firm count is an estimate derived at the 23832 ancestor level and may include firms whose economics differ from the six-digit lens.
Residential repaint, commercial maintenance, industrial coatings, and new-build subcontracting have different repeat profiles.
e.change_evidence: A representative sample of 130 dataset-identified LMM firms coded for external revenue, repeat-customer share, owner dependence, crew continuity, licensing, customer concentration, and standalone financial records would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Most LMM firms in this industry are operating contractors selling an outsourced on-site service, and the official definition explicitly includes maintenance, repairs, alterations, and new work. Eligibility is reduced for owner-centric firms without transferable crews or systems, firms overwhelmingly dependent on one-off new construction without repeat channels, and entities with customer concentration or compliance problems that make the operating business nontransferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23832 ($11,899,300,304) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23832 ($34,036,198,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23832. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $172,155 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238320); class <5: 28,272 firms, avg revenue/firm $275,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $21,900 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,360 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,057,531 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $84,179 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,516 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,269,611 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $180,661 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,552 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,689,278 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $532,467 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 119 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,283,563 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,092,172 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $124,524,818 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,912,176 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 130

caveats: The range assumes no discontinuous, broadly deployable robotics breakthrough for irregular occupied sites within five years.
Large uniform industrial or new-build surfaces may automate faster than residential and maintenance work.
This primitive concerns operator requirement, not the number of field labor hours, which may still fall modestly through tools and workflow improvements.
o.change_evidence: Field evidence from robotic-painting vendors, large painting contractors, and property managers showing the share of actual square footage completed without contractor crews, plus five-year customer adoption plans for self-service and autonomous application, would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Almost all year-five painting quantity still requires an accountable operator to inspect site conditions, prepare and protect surfaces, select materials and application methods, coordinate access and crews, control quality and safety, and physically apply coatings or wall coverings. Software can improve intake, visualization, estimating, scheduling, and documentation, while limited mechanization or customer self-service can remove a small share, but ordinary occupied and variable jobs are not software-deliverable.

caveats: The AGC evidence concerns tariff cost pass-through across construction, the opposite direction of an AI cost saving and a broader population.
BizBuySell evidence is broad small-business transaction and pricing sentiment rather than painting contract economics.
Retention will vary sharply between homeowner quotes, commercial maintenance agreements, unit-price contracts, and competitively bid subcontracts.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level analysis from acquired painting firms linking gross margin and realized price to bid type, renewal or rebid cadence, customer segment, win rate, competitor count, and documented AI-driven cost changes would materially tighten retained-benefit estimates.
q.rationale: AI savings on fixed-price work can initially accrue to the operator, especially when faster quoting, scheduling, and administration reduce overhead without changing the contracted deliverable. Over five years, competitive rebids, customer procurement, subcontract negotiations, and renewal repricing share part of that benefit. Broad construction evidence that firms both pass costs through and sometimes absorb them shows that contracts and competition transmit—but do not instantly transmit—all economics; fragmented local markets and service-quality differentiation leave room for retention.

rho.bridge: The 2026 AGC/Sage survey covers 951 construction firms across sizes and trades, not NAICS 238320 LMM contractors: 45% reported AI in office and administrative functions, 23% in estimating, and 20% in design or preconstruction. Census BTOS is nationally representative but cross-industry and reports 17%-20% current AI use and 20%-23% expected use, with less than 20% use among firms with four or fewer employees. I use these adoption levels as evidence that exposed digital workflows are already implementable, then discount for painting-firm size, fragmented systems, field-data quality, review needs, and change management. AGC's larger and more digitally mature membership likely biases implementation upward; broad Census data may bias it either way for the LMM band.
caveats: AGC respondents are not a probability sample of painting contractors and may skew larger or more technology-forward.
Census changed its core AI question in late 2025 from production use to any business function, limiting comparability.
Reported tool use is not equivalent to full operational implementation of exposed labor opportunity.
rho.change_evidence: Painting-contractor cohort data from estimating/field-service vendors showing baseline hours, feature activation, sustained weekly use, error rates, and headcount or avoided-hire outcomes for AI-assisted estimating, scheduling, CRM, and accounting over at least 12 months would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Within five years, a majority—but not all—of the exposed office and coordination opportunity should be operationally implemented. Quoting, document drafting, lead response, scheduling assistance, bookkeeping support, and knowledge retrieval are deployable now, but integration with job-cost data, human review of site conditions, safety, customer exceptions, and adoption by small operators constrain realization.

caveats: BizBuySell transactions are voluntarily broker-reported and cover broad small-business categories, not this NAICS/EBITDA lens.
Seller surveys measure intentions and motives rather than completed control transfers.
Owner age, succession readiness, and transferability for the eligible painting-firm population are unobserved.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-control-transaction counts for NAICS 238320 from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, BizBuySell broker records, SBA 7(a) acquisition-purpose loans, and state contractor-license ownership changes, reconciled to a contemporaneous eligible-firm denominator, would materially revise the probability.
s5.rationale: A meaningful minority of eligible firms should undergo a qualifying control transfer in five years. Broad small-business market evidence shows an active transaction market, retirement as a material seller motive, and brokers expecting more owners to come to market; however, many owner exits never become transferable sales, and no observed denominator-based transfer rate exists for LMM painting contractors. The range therefore discounts listing intentions, failed deals, internal handoffs, closures, and non-control transactions.

principal_driver: Repeat, physical painting and maintenance demand supports a durable operator while AI can remove friction from estimating and administrative workflows.
principal_weakness: The AI-exposed share is limited by craft-heavy payroll, and transferability plus five-year control-transfer frequency are not directly observed for LMM painting firms.
